content: Interest rate parity (IRP) is a fundamental theory stating that the difference in interest rates between two countries equals the difference between forward and spot exchange rates. Covered IRP holds almost perfectly due to arbitrage: if U.S. rates are 5% and euro rates are 3%, the forward dollar-euro rate must reflect a 2% premium to prevent risk-free profit. Uncovered IRP suggests expected future spot rates should equal forward rates, but empirical evidence shows persistent deviations—this is the forward premium puzzle. IRP matters for carry trade strategies where investors borrow in low-rate currencies to invest in high-rate currencies, profiting from the spread but exposed to exchange rate risk.
source: Journal of International Money and Finance
